rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction. Tert-butanol is protonated by a strong acid (sulfuric acid) and loses water to form a stable tert-butyl carbocation. This carbocation is then trapped by the nucleophilic nitrogen of acetonitrile, forming a nitrilium ion intermediate. Subsequent hydrolysis of the nitrilium ion yields the final product, N-tert-butylacetamide. The reactants are simple, acyclic, and clearly demonstrate the core transformation: carbocation generation, nitrile trapping, and hydrolysis to an amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with reactions involving aromatic rings, complex ring systems, or other functional groups. The core transformation is clearly the formation of a C-N bond from a carbocation and a nitrile, followed by hydrolysis to an amide, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction. It avoids motifs common in other named reactions like rearrangements (e.g., Beckmann, Pinacol) or additions to carbonyls.
